results.question: Is tirilazad effective for treatment of aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage?
results.atomic_props:
Key medical claims to verify:
- tirilazad is effective in treating aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage
results.retrieved_context:
Document [1] (Title: PubMed PMID:9509802)  This study used data from a multinational phase III randomized, double-blind, vehicle-controlled trial to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of tirilazad mesylate (Freedox) in the treatment of aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage. 
Document [2] (Title: PubMed PMID:8592224)  The benefits of treatment with tirilazad were predominantly shown in men rather than in women. 
Document [3] (Title: PubMed PMID:20166088)  Delayed cerebral ischaemia is a significant contributor to poor outcome (death or disability) in patients with aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH). 
Document [4] (Title: PubMed PMID:10350245)  Findings from previous multicenter clinical trials have suggested that tirilazad mesylate, a synthetic nonhormonal 21-aminosteroid, might be effective in preventing delayed cerebral ischemia following subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH). The use of other potentially effective rescue therapies (that is, hypervolemia, hemodilution, and induced hypertension) to counteract vasospasm may have been responsible for these contrasting observations between the two groups. 
Document [5] (Title: PubMed PMID:7714603)  No serious side effects of tirilazad treatment were identified at any of the three doses, despite close monitoring of hepatic and cardiac toxicity. We conclude that tirilazad mesylate is safe in SAH patients at doses up to 6 mg/kg per day for up to 10 days and is a promising drug for the treatment of patients with aneurysmal SAH. 
Document [6] (Title: PubMed PMID:8627581)  In humans, tirilazad is cleared almost exclusively via hepatic elimination. 
Document [7] (Title: PubMed PMID:8739825)  Tirilazad mesylate is a membrane lipid peroxidation inhibitor being evaluated for the treatment of patients with subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH); phenobarbital may be administered to these patients for seizure prophylaxis. 
Document [8] (Title: PubMed PMID:10605541)  There is good evidence for the benefit of nimodipine in aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage. 
Document [9] (Title: PubMed PMID:20175798)  Rivastigmine has been shown to be effective for patients with mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease. Its effect on cognitive impairment after aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage has not been previously studied. The aim of the study is to evaluate the efficacy and safety of rivastigmine 3 mg/day over 12 weeks in patients with aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage and persistent cognitive impairment. Baseline cholinergic dysfunction (with pupillometry) was assessed for relationship with treatment efficacy. 
Document [10] (Title: PubMed PMID:17290176)  Cerebral vasospasm after aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) is associated with the volume and location of subarachnoid blood clots. 
Document [11] (Title: PubMed PMID:23690884)  The approach of Bayesian neural networks with fuzzy logic inferences was applied to data from five trials of Tirilazad for aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (3551 patients).

reasoning_traces.step_by_step_thinking: The evidence suggests that tirilazad is effective for treatment of aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage, as shown in documents [1], [2], [3], [4], [5], and [6]. Therefore, the answer is A. Yes.

results.question: Is dexamethasone recommended for treatment of intracerebral hemorrhage?
results.atomic_props:
Key medical claims to verify:
- dexamethasone is used to treat intracerebral hemorrhage
- dexamethasone can reduce brain edema
results.retrieved_context:
Document [1] (Title: PubMed PMID:15500570)  Dexamethasone was evaluated for the treatment of intracerebral hemorrhage using a rat model of cerebral hematoma induced by intracerebral injection of collagenase. 
Document [2] (Title: PubMed PMID:3574383)  To evaluate the efficacy of dexamethasone for treatment of primary supratentorial intracerebral hemorrhage, we studied 93 patients 40 to 80 years old, using a double-blind randomized block design. In the light of the absence of a demonstrable beneficial effect and the presence of a significant harmful effect, current practices of using dexamethasone for treatment of primary supratentorial hemorrhage should be reconsidered. 
Document [3] (Title: PubMed PMID:28583496)  Dexamethasone is currently recommended for routine prophylaxis against postoperative nausea and vomiting after tonsillectomy procedures. 
Document [4] (Title: PubMed PMID:19887028)  These results indicate that a 1-mg/kg dose of dexamethasone is beneficial for treatment of intracerebral hemorrhage, particularly if administered early after the hemorrhagic insult. 
Document [5] (Title: PubMed PMID:24940109)  Based on the evidence available to date, the Alberta Provincial cns Tumour Team makes these recommendations: Treatment with dexamethasone is recommended for symptom relief in adult patients with primary high-grade glioma and cerebral edema.After surgery, a maximum dose of 16 mg daily, administered in 4 equal doses, is recommended for symptomatic patients. 
Document [6] (Title: PubMed PMID:23981692)  The primary outcome measure is presence of intracerebral hemorrhage growth by 24 ± 3 hours, defined as either >33% or >6 ml increase from baseline, and will be adjusted for baseline intracerebral hemorrhage volume. 
Document [7] (Title: PubMed PMID:23037177)  Treatment of intracerebral hemorrhage is often pointless, although considerable effort has been devoted to developing treatments for ischemic stroke. These results suggest that SPZ is a potentially effective therapeutic approach for intracerebral hemorrhage as the effect of SPZ was initiated for either 1 h or 3 d post-intracerebral hemorrhage. 
Document [8] (Title: PubMed PMID:11898571)  Treatment of intracerebral hemorrhage is based on blood pressure control, and, in selected cases, surgical evacuation of clot. This review examines the diagnosis and intensive care management of patients with intracerebral or subarachnoid hemorrhage, and reviews some of the newer therapies for treatment of these disorders. 
Document [9] (Title: PubMed PMID:21817157)  Therefore, the use of intratympanic dexamethasone is recommended only for subsequent or salvage treatment of idiopathic sudden sensorineural hearing loss after systemic steroid treatment. 
Document [10] (Title: PubMed PMID:17938297)  Evidence-based guidelines are presented for the diagnosis of intracerebral hemorrhage, the management of increased arterial blood pressure and intracranial pressure, the treatment of medical complications of intracerebral hemorrhage, and the prevention of recurrent intracerebral hemorrhage. Recommendations for various surgical approaches for treatment of spontaneous intracerebral hemorrhage are presented. 
Document [11] (Title: PubMed PMID:26279658)  Generally, definitive treatment of brain arteriovenous malformations (BAVM) presenting with hemorrhage is recommended to prevent recurrent hemorrhage. 
Document [12] (Title: PubMed PMID:34469757)  Intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) is a severe stroke subtype with high disability and mortality, and no effective treatment is available.

reasoning_traces.step_by_step_thinking: The evidence suggests that dexamethasone is not recommended for treatment of intracerebral hemorrhage, as there is no demonstrable beneficial effect and significant harmful effects have been observed. Therefore, the answer is B - No.

results.question: Is airplane stroke syndrome a common disease.
results.atomic_props:
Key medical claims to verify:
- airplane stroke syndrome is a recognized condition
- it occurs in people who fly long distances
results.retrieved_context:
Document [1] (Title: PubMed PMID:11914416)  "Economy class" stroke syndrome may be underdiagnosed and is an eminently preventable cause of stroke. 
Document [2] (Title: PubMed PMID:27634705)  The occurrence of an acute stroke syndrome during a long-distance flight demands critical decisions from the crew and attendant physicians. Endovascular treatment of a severe stroke occurring in a commercial airplane over the Sahara. 
Document [3] (Title: PubMed PMID:31781974)  High altitude headache is a common neurological symptom that is associated with ascent to high altitude. 
Document [4] (Title: PubMed PMID:12368703)  Stroke is the most common neurologic complication of sickle cell disease. Acute chest syndrome (ACS) is a known risk factor for stroke in this population. Patients with acute neurologic deterioration in conjunction with ACS should be evaluated thoroughly for other causes of central nervous system disease including infectious/parainfectious processes as well as stroke. 
Document [5] (Title: PubMed PMID:23412285)  Survival after an airplane disaster is rare. We describe the injuries of survivors of an airplane accident and present a common mechanism of trauma for victims. 
Document [6] (Title: PubMed PMID:29953035)  Childhood ischemic stroke is attributed to vasculopathy with moyamoya syndrome. 
Document [7] (Title: PubMed PMID:34247053)  Ischemic stroke and hemorrhagic stroke are the most common sequelae of the Moyamoya variants [Moyamoya disease (MMD) and syndrome (MMS)]. 
Document [8] (Title: PubMed PMID:23855070)  Airplane headache is flight-related and appears during airplane landing and/or takeoff without accompanying symptoms. 
Document [9] (Title: PubMed PMID:11848316)  Recurrent stroke seems to be common in patients with APS and a first stroke, recurrent stroke and vascular dementia are feared consequences of APS. 
Document [10] (Title: PubMed PMID:29179528)  POEMS syndrome is a rare multi-systemic disease characterized by polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, monoclonal gammopathy, and skin changes. Arterial or venous thrombosis is a less-common complication of POEMS syndrome. The common clinical manifestations of POEMS syndrome were rather non-specific in patients with ischemic stroke compared with those of patients without ischemic stroke.

reasoning_traces.step_by_step_thinking: The evidence suggests that airplane stroke syndrome is not a common disease, and the absence of explicit evidence supports this answer.
